reason: Both answers correctly identify the core legal picture: CFAA is a weak flank post-hiQ, breach-of-contract/ToS is the dominant risk (correctly noting hiQ ultimately lost on that theory), CCPA/CPRA data-broker exposure is real, and public data isn't risk-free. Both cite the LinkedIn v. Proxycurl enforcement pattern and give practical mitigation advice, landing on similarly structured, actionable verdicts. The differentiator is B's inclusion of genuinely new, decision-relevant risk vectors that A omits: BIPA biometric exposure if profile photos are stored/matched (with Clearview precedent), FTC Section 5 unfairness exposure, and an explicit naming of the Power Ventures precedent for CFAA revival post-C&D. A's advantage is source links, but these add verifiability rather than new substantive analysis. Neither has a disqualifying error; B is marginally more complete.
